metadata.title: Build a Bloch Sphere Sim with Claude Code
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = sphere and |up> state. CRIMSON = |+> equatorial state. INK for all Text.

props.topic: HUMANITARIANS AI · Build a Bloch Sphere Sim with Claude Code

beats.narration_text: Humanitarians AI — Claude Code, Manim, and the physics, taken apart.

props.command: Build a Bloch Sphere Sim with Claude Code
props.segment: Build a Bloch Sphere Sim with Claude Code

beats.narration_text: Here is why Build a Bloch Sphere Sim with Claude Code belongs in your working knowledge. The systems, policies, and tools shaped by it affect decisions at your level — whether or not you understand the mechanism. Knowing the underlying logic gives you the frame; missing it means reasoning from output with no access to the inputs.

props.command: Build a Bloch Sphere Sim with Claude Code
props.segment: Build a Bloch Sphere Sim with Claude Code

beats.narration_text: The prompt. Claude Code terminal: claude — draw a Bloch sphere cross-section in the x-z plane. Place three states: spin-up at the north pole, spin-down at the south pole, and the plus state at the equator. Mark each with its theta angle. The parameterization uses theta over two — not theta. Specify: the half-angle formula and the three states.

props.command:
claude "draw Bloch sphere cross-section (x-z plane):
  |ψ⟩ = cos(θ/2)|↑⟩ + sin(θ/2)|↓⟩
  |↑⟩ at θ=0  (north pole)
  |+⟩ at θ=π/2 (equator)
  |↓⟩ at θ=π  (south pole)"

beats.narration_text: The CLI writes it. Read before running. Find the parameterization line. It should read: cosine of theta over two — theta divided by two — for the spin-up amplitude. If it reads cosine of theta, the states land in the wrong positions. Spin-up at the north pole is theta equals zero, so cosine of zero over two equals one. That is the only amplitude that puts probability one at the north pole. Thirty seconds. Catch it before the render.

props.code:
# bloch.py  —  Claude Code output
import numpy as np

def bloch_vector(theta, phi=0):
    # ← half-angle: θ/2 (not θ)
    x = np.sin(theta) * np.cos(phi)
    z = np.cos(theta)
    return x, z

# |↑⟩: theta=0   → (x=0, z=1) north pole
# |+⟩: theta=π/2 → (x=1, z=0) equator
# |↓⟩: theta=π   → (x=0, z=-1) south pole
props.title: bloch.py

beats.narration_text: Run it. Two checks. First: the teal arrow at the north pole. It points straight up — theta equals zero. Measuring spin along z gives spin-up with probability cosine-squared of zero equals one. One hundred percent. Second: the crimson arrow at the equator. It points along x. Measuring along z now gives expectation value cosine of pi over two — which is zero. Equal odds of up and down. Two different arrows, two testable numbers.

Iterate: claude — update bloch dot py — add the minus state at theta equals pi over two, phi equals pi, on the opposite side of the equator. Label it vertical bar minus right-angle-bracket. One diff, one re-run. Prompt, read, run, check, change. The Bloch sphere was the vehicle. The loop is the takeaway.

props.command:
claude "update bloch.py:
  → add |−⟩ at θ=π/2, φ=π  (opposite equator)
  → label it |−⟩"
